title: Gilding with gum ammoniac, laying gold leaf on paper
subtitle: The medieval mordant-gilding technique for illuminated work
excerpt: Gum ammoniac is a sticky resin dissolved in water and used as a mordant for gold leaf. The method predates raised gesso gilding and produces a flat, burnished gold surface on paper or vellum. It appears in manuscript borders and pen-flourished initials from the Carolingian period onward.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on gilding: Johnston describes both gum ammoniac and gesso gilding in detail, with specific guidance on preparing the mordant, laying the gold, and burnishing. The book is in the public domain. Johnston's description remains the standard reference for the technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: Gilding that uses a sticky adhesive (the mordant) to hold gold leaf flat on the page. Contrasts with gesso gilding where the gold is raised on a built-up relief.
Loose gold leaf mounted on tissue paper for handling. Pressed face-down onto the tacky mordant and then peeled away, leaving the gold behind.

content.text: is the simplest form of gold leaf work. The gum is dissolved in water, painted onto the paper, and allowed to dry. When you breathe on the dried surface, the moisture reactivates the adhesive for a few seconds, long enough to press a sheet of
onto it. The result is a flat gold surface that can be burnished to a mirror finish.

items.name: Gum ammoniac (gilding mordant)
items.notes: Sold as a liquid or as dry tears dissolved in water. The dissolved liquid should be consistency of thin cream.

items.name: Transfer gold (23.5 carat, loose leaf on tissue)
items.notes: Transfer gold is easier to handle than loose gold. Available in books of 25 leaves, each on tissue.

items.name: Soft brush (conservation)
items.notes: For painting the mordant. A fine detail brush for small areas; a broader brush for large areas.

items.name: Watercolour paper, hot-press
items.notes: A smooth surface gives better gold adhesion than cold-press. Hot-press also burnishes more evenly.

items.name: Burnishing tool
items.notes: An agate or haematite burnisher. The back of a smooth spoon or a very smooth nail also works at home scale.

items.name: Soft paper shield
items.notes: A piece of smooth paper held over the gold while burnishing, to prevent the burnisher scratching the gold surface.

content.text: Apply the gum ammoniac to the design area with the soft brush. Work in thin coats rather than a thick wet layer, thick applications crack as they dry. Allow to dry fully: in a warm room this takes 10-15 minutes. The dried mordant is pale, slightly waxy, and barely visible. Apply a second coat if the first appears patchy when dry.

Hold the paper 3-5 cm from your mouth and breathe steadily onto the mordanted area for two to three seconds. The surface must be damp, not wet, the aim is moisture from breath, not drops. Immediately place the transfer gold tissue face-down onto the mordant. Press firmly through the tissue with a finger for fifteen seconds, covering the full gilded area. Peel the tissue away slowly from one corner.

The first application rarely achieves full coverage, gaps and pinholes are normal. Breathe on the area again and apply a second piece of transfer gold over the gaps. Gum ammoniac accepts multiple gold layers. A third pass is sometimes needed on edges.

Allow the gold to sit undisturbed for 30 minutes. Place the paper shield over the gold and burnish through the shield with a smooth circular motion. Increase pressure gradually, burnishing too hard too early tears the gold. Move to direct burnishing (without the paper shield) once the gold has consolidated, working in small circles. The gold should achieve a mirror finish.
